text: Runners with active plantar fasciitis exhibit less forefoot extension, more midfoot inversion, and more midfoot extension compared to those with resolved plantar fasciitis, suggesting a lower, more flexible arch during dynamic movement.
specific_value: P = 0.02 (forefoot extension), P < 0.01 (midfoot inversion and extension)

text: Static measures of foot structure (arch height index, arch rigidity index, arch drop) and ankle motion do not differ between runners with active plantar fasciitis, resolved plantar fasciitis, and uninjured controls, meaning static assessments alone may miss biomechanical risk factors.
Runners with active plantar fasciitis demonstrate greater sagittal knee range of motion during running than both resolved and uninjured controls, indicating possible compensatory mechanics.
Forefoot and midfoot dynamics during gait — not static arch measurements — are the key biomechanical variables to monitor when assessing plantar fasciitis risk and status in runners.
Runners with resolved plantar fasciitis show foot mechanics similar to uninjured controls, suggesting that biomechanical differences associated with plantar fasciitis may normalize after recovery.
